key_departures.impact: Released as a post-June 1 designation. Cousins leaves behind $22.5M in dead money in 2026 and $12.5M in 2027 after a disastrous two-year tenure. Benched in 2025 for Michael Penix Jr. His departure was fully expected and planned for — the Falcons already had Penix and pivoted immediately to Tua Tagovailoa as a bridge. Net cap save of $2.1M in 2026. Departure does not create a new need; it was the plan.

Signed a 3-year, $33M deal ($23M guaranteed, max $39M) with Saints. In three seasons with Atlanta, he started all 51 games, posting 380 tackles and 12.5 sacks. DC Jeff Ulbrich said replacing him would 'take more than one human being.' One of the most damaging departures for the Falcons this offseason.

Signed 3-year deal worth up to $21M with $10.12M guaranteed with Bills. Was the primary slot/nickel corner in Jeff Ulbrich's defense and performed well as a starter when Billy Bowman Jr. was injured. A meaningful depth loss at a position of need.

Signed 1-year deal worth up to $7.3M with $4.3M guaranteed with Eagles. A 2022 second-round pick who never fully broke out (6 sacks best season), but provided reliable rotational value in Ulbrich's defense. His departure was effectively sealed when the Falcons signed Azeez Ojulari. Adds some pass-rush depth concern.
Signed 1-year, $10.5M deal with Jets. Spent three seasons with the Falcons as a versatile interior lineman. His departure deepens the IDL need the Falcons already had.
Signed 2-year, $12.25M deal with Cardinals. Was Bijan Robinson's primary backup for three seasons. Leaves a notable gap behind Robinson with no established secondary RB on roster.

Released by Falcons. Was scheduled to carry an $18.4M cap hit in 2026; Falcons save $7.4M taking dead money now. Was one of the WR2 options in 2025 but underperformed badly. Departure contributes to WR room being nearly bare before FA additions.
Released with $3.3M cap savings. Was a key special teams ace and WR depth piece. Departure adds to WR and special teams thinning.
Hit free agency. Veteran rotational presence combined with Ebiketie for 5.5 sacks in 2025. Departure contributes to overall EDGE depth concern alongside the James Pearce Jr. legal situation.

signings.analysis: Franchise tag keeps Pitts for 2026. He finished 2025 second among TEs in receptions (88) and yards (928), was named AP second-team All-Pro, and added a career-high 5 TDs. Coach Kevin Stefanski explicitly values the TE position; long-term extension talks are ongoing with a July 15 deadline. Tag fully secures the TE1 role. TE is not a draft need. Austin Hooper added as TE2 depth. Position is fully covered for 2026.

Veteran minimum deal. Miami is paying his $54M 2026 guaranteed salary, so Falcons get him for pennies. Tagovailoa will compete with Michael Penix Jr. for the starting role — Penix is recovering from a torn ACL suffered in Week 11 2025. This is a bridge/insurance signing, not a long-term solution. The 1-year deal at minimum clearly signals he is not the answer; QB remains a need in the sense that Penix must prove himself healthy and ready. No long-term commitment. Need is only partially addressed: Falcons have capable QBs in the building, but no certainty at the position going forward.

2-year, $15M deal ($10M guaranteed, up to $17M) per Spotrac and ESPN. A former first-round pick (16th overall, 2022) who underperformed in Philadelphia (37 catches in 34 games), but he was rarely targeted behind A.J. Brown and DeVonta Smith. At 25, he has genuine upside and a 4.43 40-yard dash. Expected to compete for the WR2 role vacated by Mooney. The 2-year commitment and $10M guarantee show real investment, but given his recent production ceiling, this is a partial solution. WR2 role is plausibly addressed, but the room still needs another viable weapon. Team may still draft a WR.

Terms not fully disclosed; reported as a low-cost reunion deal per multiple sources. Had 39 catches for 313 yards and 2 TDs with Chicago in 2025. Returns as a familiar face — a former UDFA who spent 4 seasons in Atlanta originally. Slot/gadget specialist and a known commodity for new GM Ian Cunningham (both were with Chicago). Projects as WR3/4. Does not address the WR2 need on its own; provides genuine depth around Drake London. One-year deal signals pure depth.

1-year, $3.25M deal. Reunited with HC Kevin Stefanski, who coached him in Cleveland. Made back-to-back Pro Bowls in Atlanta in 2018-19. At 31, this is a late-career depth and matchup piece to play alongside Kyle Pitts in 12 personnel sets that Stefanski loves. Well below starter money at the position. Does not address a need — TE was already resolved via the Pitts franchise tag. This is pure 'Stefanski loves TEs' depth.

1-year deal, exact terms not fully disclosed per Spotrac; reported as a low-value 1-year deal similar to his prior $3-4M Eagles deal. A former Giants 2nd-round pick (2021) with 22 career sacks who has been repeatedly derailed by injuries — appeared in only 3 games in 2025 due to a hamstring injury. When healthy he's flashed top-end pass-rush ability (8 sacks rookie year, 6 in 2023). At 25, he has genuine upside but the 1-year minimum-range deal reflects injury risk. A high-upside depth/rotational signing that minimally addresses EDGE given the group is still thin. James Pearce Jr. faces 5 felony charges and likely won't play in 2026. EDGE remains a real need.

Terms not formally disclosed; reported as a low-cost signing per ESPN/Schefter. Had 9.5 sacks in 2023 before tearing his Achilles, missing all of 2024, and posting just 2 sacks in a limited 2025 (43% snap rate). An eight-year veteran with 35 career sacks and 11 forced fumbles — the most proven of the Falcons' EDGE additions. But at 30 and coming off a major injury, ceiling is limited. His signing alongside Ojulari and Thomas partially fills the Ebiketie/Floyd hole at edge rusher rotation, but the group still lacks a true starter-caliber presence opposite Jalon Walker. EDGE is still a need for the draft.

1-year, $3.1M deal per Adam Schefter. A 2022 third-round pick by Arizona who played the last two seasons in Cleveland under Kevin Stefanski. Had 2.5 sacks and 30 tackles in 2025 as a rotational player. Versatile DE who can play standing up or with hand in the dirt. A Stefanski familiar face addition — not a starter-caliber player but a solid depth piece who can contribute rotational pass-rush. Low cost, low ceiling. Minimally addresses EDGE need.

1-year deal, worth up to $3M per CBS Sports. In four seasons with Houston, he had 206 total tackles, 4 sacks, 2 forced fumbles, and 1 interception, but missed significant time with injuries in both 2024 and 2025. At 25, he has the athleticism and traits the Falcons covet — DC Jeff Ulbrich has shown a pattern of developing young athletic linebackers (see Divine Deablo). He will compete for the starting LB2 spot beside Deablo. But given his injury history, this is far from a guaranteed starter solution. LB remains a legitimate draft need; Kaden Elliss's departure left a massive hole.

1-year deal, terms not fully disclosed. A former Dolphins 3rd-round pick in 2022 who never broke through defensively (4 defensive snaps but 100+ special teams snaps in 2025 with Arizona). Primarily a coverage teams specialist. At 25 with good speed, he has some developmental LB upside but cannot be counted on as a defensive starter. His value is in fixing Atlanta's special teams catastrophe in 2025. Does not address the Elliss-sized LB hole. LB need unchanged at starter level.

Re-signed. Terms not fully disclosed. Had 5 sacks and 7 TFLs in 13 games (5 starts) in 2025 — the Falcons' most productive nose tackle last season. Retaining him was the first re-signing the new staff made, speaking to his value. However, with David Onyemata leaving and Chris Williams added, IDL depth is still a concern behind starters Dorlus, Orhorhoro, and Harrison. London's retention minimally stabilizes the IDL room but does not resolve a position group that likely needs a draft pick.

1-year, $2M deal. An undrafted free agent out of Wagner who spent the last two seasons with the Chicago Bears where GM Ian Cunningham was in the front office. Had 4 sacks and 37 tackles in 2 seasons with Chicago. A depth rotational piece to partially offset Onyemata's departure. Does not move the needle on IDL as a meaningful need — the position still needs meaningful talent infusion.

1-year deal, minimal terms not fully disclosed per reports. A veteran interior lineman (C/G) who will compete with Ryan Neuzil for the starting center role. Was coached by new OL coach Bill Callahan in Tennessee. A Georgia native — familiarity and staff connection drove the signing. Not a guaranteed starter; Neuzil is the incumbent at center and Lindstrom/Bergeron are cemented at guard. IOL is not a primary need but Neuzil has question marks. Low-risk depth addition.

2-year, $9M deal ($4M guaranteed). The Falcons went through three kickers in 2025 (Younghoe Koo, Parker Romo, Zane Gonzalez) in one of the league's worst special teams seasons. Folk made 96.6% of his field goals in 2025 with the Jets — best rate in the NFL — including 7/7 from 50+. The 2-year deal is a real commitment. Special teams coordinator Craig Aukerman had Folk with the Titans in 2023. K need fully resolved.

3-year, $9M deal ($5M fully guaranteed). A 2020 All-Pro punter (48.7 yards per punt average) who has performed well for both the Patriots and Dolphins. Averaged 47.7 yards per punt in 2025 with Miami. The Falcons needed a punter upgrade — Bradley Pinion was their only real bright spot on special teams but they are moving on. 3-year commitment is significant for a punter and signals they view this as a long-term fix. P need fully addressed.

Low-cost extension. An undrafted free agent from Southern Miss who appeared in 26 games (1 start) over 2024-2025 on the Falcons roster/practice squad. His one extended stint in 2025 did not go well. A depth/practice squad type. Does not address CB starter need whatsoever.
Exclusive rights free agent tender. A depth piece along the interior. Standard roster management, does not meaningfully address IDL need.

Restructured to avoid a tolled contract year after missing all of 2025 on the PUP list with knee injuries. His 4th year didn't count, so he returns in 2026 at just over $1M. A former 2nd-round pick who showed flashes early in his career but has played just 9 games since his rookie season. If healthy, he's a developmental upside play and real depth piece at LB. Cannot be counted on as a starter given injury history. Minimally moves the needle on the LB need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Tua Tagovailoa (1-year minimum) bridges the QB situation while Michael Penix Jr. recovers from ACL surgery. The Falcons have two legitimate QBs in the building for 2026. However, the 1-year minimum signals no long-term commitment to Tua — this is a bridge year. Penix's health and readiness remain uncertain. QB drops from critical to moderate need; they may still want a developmental QB late in the draft but QB1/QB2 is covered for 2026.

Jahan Dotson (2yr/$15M) is a genuine WR2 investment with upside, and Zaccheaus returns as WR3/4 depth. Drake London anchors the room. But the WR room is still below average — Dotson has underperformed expectations, and the room lacks a true WR2 proven commodity. WR drops from critical to high need. An early-to-mid round WR in the draft remains a strong possibility and is still needed.

Kyle Pitts franchise tagged ($15.045M guaranteed, 2nd-team All-Pro in 2025) plus Austin Hooper added as TE2. Kevin Stefanski's offense is built around the TE. TE is fully resolved for 2026. Long-term extension talks underway with July 15 deadline. No TE draft need.

Kaden Elliss (3yr/$33M) departed to the Saints — a genuine starter and defensive cornerstone who DC Jeff Ulbrich said could not be replaced by one person. The Falcons signed Christian Harris (injury history, limited recent production), Channing Tindall (primarily a special teams player), and retained Troy Andersen (missed all of 2025). Divine Deablo is the only cemented starter. The LB group is severely undermanned. LB escalates to critical need — should be a top-3 priority in the 2026 NFL Draft.

Three EDGE additions (Ojulari, Ebukam, Cameron Thomas) were all 1-year depth deals, none approaching starter money. Jalon Walker is the only cemented starter opposite. James Pearce Jr. faces 5 felony charges and is effectively unavailable for 2026. Arnold Ebiketie and Leonard Floyd both departed. The floor has been raised with depth rotation (Walker + Ojulari + Ebukam + Thomas + Trice), but there is no elite presence opposite Walker. EDGE remains a top draft priority — drops slightly from tier 1 because of the rotational depth added, but still a high need for a true starter/long-term piece.

Dee Alford's departure to Buffalo (3yr/$21M) removes their best nickel/slot corner. A.J. Terrell Jr., Mike Hughes, and Billy Bowman Jr. remain under contract as starters, but losing Alford deepens the CB depth concern. The only CB addition was Natrone Brooks (a minimum-deal depth piece). CB need is unchanged to slightly escalated — still a tier 2 need, particularly for a nickel/slot corner and CB2 depth.

David Onyemata departed to the Jets ($10.5M deal). LaCale London was re-signed (valuable) and Chris Williams added on a $2M deal. Starters Brandon Dorlus, Ruke Orhorhoro, and Zach Harrison remain. However, depth behind them is thin and the overall group lacks an established veteran presence. IDL is unchanged — still a meaningful need for the draft, particularly for a rotational player who can complement the young starters.

Tyler Allgeier's departure to Arizona (2yr/$12.25M) leaves Bijan Robinson with no established backup on the roster. Nathan Carter is the only depth option but played minimally in 2025. The Falcons need to add a capable RB2 — either in free agency (still ongoing) or in the draft. RB need escalates from tier 3 to tier 2.

Corey Levin (1-year minimum) added as depth/competition at center, but starter Ryan Neuzil and guards Chris Lindstrom and Matthew Bergeron are all returning. Bergeron may be due for an extension. IOL is largely intact — unchanged at tier 3. Center depth is the only concern, and Levin adds competition.

No changes at OT. Jake Matthews (restructured) and McGary return as starters. Jake Matthews' contract restructure commits the Falcons to him for two more years. The tackle room is stable — unchanged need.

key_additions.headline: Former Pro Bowl QB signs at veteran minimum as bridge starter while Michael Penix Jr. recovers from ACL
2022 first-round pick reunites with Penn State roots, projects as WR2 alongside Drake London
AP second-team All-Pro (88 rec, 928 yds, 5 TD) kept off market via franchise tag; long-term extension expected
Marietta native and Georgia Bulldog returns home on reclamation deal; has 22 career sacks when healthy
35-career-sack veteran adds pass-rush depth following departures of Ebiketie and Floyd
NFL's most accurate kicker (96.6% FG rate in 2025) ends Falcons' kicker carousel with 2-year commitment
2020 All-Pro punter replaces Bradley Pinion as Falcons overhaul their historically bad 2025 special teams
Former back-to-back Pro Bowler reunites with Kevin Stefanski as TE2 in expected 12-personnel heavy offense
Athletic 25-year-old competes for LB2 role to partially offset Kaden Elliss's departure to the Saints

key_losses.headline: Defensive cornerstone (380 tackles, 12.5 sacks in 3 ATL seasons) returns to rival Saints — DC Ulbrich called him irreplaceable
Starting nickel corner departs for big-money deal, deepening Atlanta's secondary concerns
Rotational edge rusher departs, compounding James Pearce Jr.'s legal absence and Leonard Floyd's exit
Three-year Falcons interior lineman exits for Jets, thinning an already-needy IDL room
Bijan Robinson's backup for three seasons departs, leaving the backfield with no established RB2
Planned release of $180M bust clears path for Tua/Penix era, but costs $22.5M in 2026 dead money
